explicit_text: My name is Heiji Kurokawa, born in May 1902 in Wakayama Prefecture. I came to America in 1921 at the age of 19, landing in San Francisco after a rough voyage on the Nihon Maru. I worked mainly in farming, especially grape pruning, and later managed an apartment in Fresno. Throughout my life, I experienced many challenges, including the anti-Japanese land laws and the hardships of World War II evacuation.
implicit_text: My name is Heiji, born in May 1902 in a southern Japanese prefecture known for its agriculture. I came to America in 1921 at the age of 19, landing in a major West Coast port city after a rough voyage on a Japanese passenger ship. I worked mainly in farming, especially grape pruning, and later managed an apartment in a Central California city. Throughout my life, I experienced many challenges, including the anti-Japanese land laws and the hardships of World War II evacuation.

explicit_text: I was born in Wakayama Prefecture, a farming area where my family grew citrus fruits and even exported them to Manchuria. Many people from Wakayama came to America in those days, so it was not unusual for me to follow my brother and mother here. I also organized the Wakayama Kenjin-Kai, a prefectural association for people from Wakayama living in Central California.

explicit_text: I landed in San Francisco on March 31, 1921, after nearly a month at sea on the Nihon Maru. I stayed there for three nights to pick up my luggage before heading to Fresno by train. San Francisco was my first impression of America, and I realized I had arrived in a foreign country with many tall white people and streetcars as the main transportation.

explicit_text: Before coming to America, my family was involved in growing citrus fruits which we exported to Manchuria. I had considered going there as well, but my brother wanted me to come to America to be partners. So while Manchuria was a business connection for us, America became my new home.

explicit_text: My great-grandfather was a samurai who fought in the war of 1879, and I remember he had a seiza patch on his head. My father was also an ex-samurai but was not very good at business. This samurai heritage was part of my family's history, though by my time we were farmers and immigrants.

explicit_text: I don't remember the Sino-Japanese War directly, but I learned about it from my teachers when I started school. It was part of the history taught to us, but I was too young to have personal memories of it. It was one of the conflicts that shaped Japan's modern history.

explicit_text: My family religion was Buddhism, and I was raised with that faith. However, my mother later became a believer in Tenrikyo when my sister was seriously ill. I myself was baptized as a Christian later in America, but Buddhism was my original family religion in Japan.

explicit_text: My mother became an ardent believer in Tenrikyo when my sister became seriously ill, as she was searching for a responsive god to pray to. However, when my mother returned to Japan from America, she advised us to become Christians since there was no Tenrikyo church in Northern California. So Tenrikyo played a role in my family's spiritual journey but was not my final faith.

explicit_text: When I came to America in 1921, there were still many picture brides on board the ship, as picture marriage was prohibited after 1924. People advised me to get married and bring a wife with me, but I was young and focused on education. I understood the difficulties picture brides faced, arriving to meet husbands they only knew from photos.

I understood the difficulties these brides faced, arriving to meet husbands they only knew from photos.

explicit_text: I came to America on the Nihon Maru, a small Japanese ship, in March 1921. It was the last voyage for that ship from Japan, and the journey took almost a month. The voyage was rough with storms and seasickness, but the men on board took care of me as I was young and alone.

explicit_text: After arriving in San Francisco, I took a train to Fresno where my mother was living at the time. I worked in grape farming there, pruning vines and learning from older Issei workers. Fresno was also where I managed an apartment with my wife and where we were placed in an assembly center during World War II evacuation.

explicit_text: During World War II, I was evacuated with my family and placed in a makeshift assembly center in Fresno, transported there by bus from Selma. We were only allowed to bring limited belongings, and the support from white church ladies was very comforting. After the war, rebuilding our lives was difficult, and some Japanese organizations like the Kenjin-Kai became extinct during that time.

After the war, rebuilding our lives was difficult, and some Japanese organizations like the prefectural associations became extinct during that time.

explicit_text: The Japanese Association was the main political organization for Japanese in Central California during my time. They mediated troubles between Japanese and Americans and helped maintain community order. Each area had its own Japanese Association, and they were important for support and representation.

explicit_text: Though my family originally practiced Buddhism and Tenrikyo, I became a Christian after marrying my wife, who had been attending the Methodist Church since youth. We married in a Christian church in 1932, following my mother's advice since there was no Tenrikyo church in Northern California. I was baptized by a minister and embraced Christianity as my faith in America.
